title: Heat and Cold Therapy Application
subtitle: The nurse applies heat and cold safely — cold for acute injury and swelling, heat for chronic stiffness, always with a skin barrier, a 15-20 minute limit, and frequent skin checks.

patient.name: Heat & Cold Therapy
patient.admitDate: The nurse applies heat and cold therapy using correct indications, barriers, timing, and skin monitoring.

labs.v: No higher temp to rush it.

nursesNote: The nurse applies heat and cold therapy safely and for the correct indication. Cold causes vasoconstriction and is used for acute injuries, swelling, inflammation, and bleeding (especially the first 24-48 hours), while heat causes vasodilation and relieves chronic muscle stiffness and spasm by increasing blood flow. A cloth or towel barrier is always placed between the pack and the skin, application is limited to about 15-20 minutes with recovery time between, and the skin is checked frequently for redness, pallor, blistering, or numbness. Patients with impaired sensation, poor circulation, cognitive impairment, or extremes of age are at higher risk for burns or frostbite. Heat is avoided on a fresh, swollen, or bleeding injury, and the nurse never raises the temperature to speed the effect or lets a patient lie directly on a heating device.
providerOrders: Apply cold to acute injury or swelling; apply heat to chronic stiffness
Place a cloth barrier between the pack and the skin
Check the skin frequently for redness, pallor, or numbness
Avoid heat on a fresh or bleeding injury

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about heat and cold therapy?

opts.text: Cold therapy causes vasoconstriction and reduces swelling
Heat therapy causes vasodilation and increases blood flow
A barrier is placed between the pack and the skin
Heat or cold can be left on for an hour at a time
Cold is best for an acute injury in the first 24-48 hours
The skin should be checked frequently during therapy
Heat applied to an acute injury can worsen swelling and bleeding
Patients with impaired sensation are at higher risk for burns
Prolonged cold can cause tissue damage or frostbite
Higher temperatures speed healing and are safe
Heat helps relieve chronic muscle stiffness
Therapy is limited to about 15-20 minutes with recovery time

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps to apply a cold pack

opts.text: Verify the order and check the skin and sensation
Wrap the cold pack in a cloth barrier
Apply to the area for 15-20 minutes
Check the skin periodically for pallor or numbness
Remove and allow the tissue to rewarm
Acute ankle sprain in the first 24 hours
Chronic muscle stiffness and aching
Active bleeding or fresh bruising
Stiff arthritic joints
Reducing acute swelling and inflammation
Relaxing tense muscles before stretching
A new sports injury with swelling
Promoting blood flow to a healing area

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Skin turns pale and numb during cold therapy
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Remove the cold pack immediately
Assess the skin and the patient's sensation
Allow the area to rewarm gradually
Notify the provider if changes persist
Document the finding and the response
Placing a towel between an ice pack and the skin
Applying a heating pad directly to bare skin
Limiting application to 15-20 minutes
Using heat on a fresh, swollen ankle sprain
Checking the skin every few minutes
Letting a patient lie on top of a heating pad
Using cold to reduce acute swelling
Turning the heat higher to work faster

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Basic Care and Comfort, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
